questions.stem: A client reports severe eye pain, blurred vision, and seeing halos around lights. Which interpretation by the nurse is most accurate?

options.text: The client may have acute angle-closure glaucoma and needs urgent evaluation.
This is an expected effect of routine eye fatigue.
The client is likely having a migraine and can rest at home.
These findings show that prescribed eye drops are working well.

questions.rationale: Severe eye pain with blurred vision and halos is concerning for acute angle-closure glaucoma, an ocular emergency that can threaten vision. The nurse should recognize the need for urgent ophthalmic evaluation rather than dismissing the symptoms as routine strain. Time-sensitive treatment matters for outcome.

distractorRationales.b: This option overweights This is an expected effect of routine eye fatigue, while the decisive cue is Severe eye pain with blurred vision and halos is concerning for acute angle-closure glaucoma, an ocular emergency. Treats an active warning sign as something safe to watch, so Choosing it can delay bedside safety action and leave clinical safety risk unresolved.
distractorRationales.c: The client is likely having a migraine and can rest at home would not address the priority, when the bedside priority is driven by A client reports severe eye pain, blurred vision. Moves to a lower-acuity action before the client is clinically stable, so The client may have acute angle-closure glaucoma and needs urgent evaluation better matches the nursing priority, so this option would slow the needed intervention.
distractorRationales.d: These findings show that prescribed eye drops are working well is not the best answer, when the bedside priority is driven by Eye pain plus halos and blurred vision should trigger concern for acute angle-closure glaucoma. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so It is less defensible because The client may have acute angle-closure glaucoma and needs urgent evaluation targets the problem most likely to deteriorate first.

questions.takeaway: Eye pain plus halos and blurred vision should trigger concern for acute angle-closure glaucoma.

questions.stem: A client receiving IV antibiotics suddenly develops wheezing, widespread hives, and swelling of the lips. Which action should the nurse take first?

options.text: Stop the infusion and prepare to administer epinephrine.
Obtain a full allergy history before intervening.
Slow the infusion to reduce the reaction.
Document the event and continue monitoring.

questions.rationale: Wheezing, hives, and lip swelling indicate anaphylaxis. The causative infusion should be stopped immediately and epinephrine readied or administered per protocol because airway compromise and shock can develop quickly. Delaying definitive treatment can be fatal.

distractorRationales.b: The problem with Obtain a full allergy history before intervening is that it does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, while the decisive cue is A client receiving IV antibiotics suddenly develops wheezing, widespread hives. That mismatch risks slower recognition of oxygenation or ventilation and postpones the needed nursing response.
distractorRationales.c: Slow the infusion to reduce the reaction fits a lower-risk interpretation, despite the clinical warning in A client receiving IV antibiotics suddenly develops wheezing, widespread hives. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so The priority is Stop the infusion and prepare to administer epinephrine, not a step that delays stabilization of oxygenation or ventilation.
distractorRationales.d: The reasoning trap in Document the event and continue monitoring is that it treats an active warning sign as something safe to watch, and misses that The causative infusion should be stopped immediately and epinephrine readied or administered per protocol because airway compromise. The correct path is Stop the infusion and prepare to administer epinephrine, which matches the priority cues instead of a secondary concern.

questions.takeaway: When anaphylaxis begins, stop the trigger and act for airway and circulation immediately.

questions.stem: A client with profuse diarrhea tests positive for Clostridioides difficile. Which infection-control action should the nurse implement?

options.text: Use soap and water for hand hygiene after removing gloves.
Use alcohol-based sanitizer alone because it is faster.
Place the client on droplet precautions only.
Share the blood pressure cuff after wiping it with a dry towel.

questions.rationale: C. difficile spores are not reliably removed by alcohol-based hand rub alone, so soap-and-water hand hygiene is required after glove removal. Contact precautions and dedicated equipment are also important. Using only sanitizer or shared equipment increases transmission risk.

distractorRationales.b: The clinical error in Use alcohol-based sanitizer alone because it is faster is that it anchors on the wrong explanation for the client's cues, and misses that c diff spore precautions infection. That choice could make the nurse miss the window for infection or sepsis escalation while infection progression progresses.
distractorRationales.c: The problem with Place the client on droplet precautions only is that it does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, although the nurse should be guided by c diff spore precautions infection. The nurse should prioritize Use soap and water for hand hygiene after removing gloves; otherwise infection progression remains the active threat.
distractorRationales.d: The clinical error in Share the blood pressure cuff after wiping it with a dry towel is that it anchors on the wrong explanation for the client's cues, but the stem cue is c diff spore precautions infection. Use soap and water for hand hygiene after removing gloves is safer because it addresses infection progression before routine care or reassurance.

questions.takeaway: C. difficile care requires soap-and-water hand hygiene and contact precautions.

questions.stem: A client with diabetic ketoacidosis is lethargic, has dry mucous membranes, blood pressure 88/54 mmHg, and blood glucose 620 mg/dL. Which intervention should the nurse anticipate first?

options.text: Rapid isotonic fluid replacement.
Long-acting insulin by subcutaneous injection.
A potassium-restricted diet.
Sodium bicarbonate for all clients with DKA.

questions.rationale: The immediate threat in DKA is severe volume depletion and impaired perfusion. Isotonic fluid replacement is the first major intervention, followed by insulin and electrolyte management based on potassium status and response. Subcutaneous long-acting insulin and routine bicarbonate do not address the acute circulatory problem first.

distractorRationales.b: Selecting Long-acting insulin by subcutaneous injection would be unsafe, instead of responding to In DKA with hypotension and dehydration, restore circulating volume first. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so The priority is Rapid isotonic fluid replacement, not a step that delays stabilization of unstable glucose physiology.
distractorRationales.c: A potassium-restricted diet would not address the priority, even though the higher-risk finding is Isotonic fluid replacement is the first major intervention, followed by insulin and electrolyte management based on potassium status. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so Rapid isotonic fluid replacement better matches the nursing priority, so this option would slow the needed intervention.
distractorRationales.d: This option makes Sodium bicarbonate for all clients with DKA the focus, without accounting for Isotonic fluid replacement is the first major intervention, followed by insulin and electrolyte management based on potassium status. Anchors on the wrong explanation for the client's cues, so The correct path is Rapid isotonic fluid replacement, which matches the priority cues instead of a secondary concern.

questions.takeaway: In DKA with hypotension and dehydration, restore circulating volume first.

questions.stem: A client using a fentanyl patch develops a fever of 102 F (38.9 C). Which instruction should the nurse provide?

options.text: Report the fever promptly because heat can increase fentanyl absorption.
Place a heating pad over the patch to improve comfort.
Cut the patch in half if the client feels too sleepy.
Apply an extra patch if the pain feels worse.

questions.rationale: Heat and fever can increase fentanyl absorption from a transdermal patch and raise the risk of oversedation or respiratory depression. The client should report this promptly and avoid external heat sources. Cutting or doubling patches is unsafe.

distractorRationales.b: This answer choice treats Place a heating pad over the patch to improve comfort as the key issue, despite the clinical warning in Transdermal fentanyl becomes riskier with fever or heat exposure because absorption can increase. Moves to a lower-acuity action before the client is clinically stable, so The safer answer is Report the fever promptly because heat can increase fentanyl absorption because delaying airway and breathing intervention can worsen oxygenation or ventilation.

Report the fever promptly because heat can increase fentanyl absorption better matches the nursing priority, so this option would slow the needed intervention.
distractorRationales.d: The clinical error in Apply an extra patch if the pain feels worse is that it does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, but the stem cue is A client using a fentanyl patch develops a fever of 102 F (38. Report the fever promptly because heat can increase fentanyl absorption is safer because it addresses oxygenation or ventilation before routine care or reassurance.

questions.takeaway: Transdermal fentanyl becomes riskier with fever or heat exposure because absorption can increase.

questions.stem: A client hospitalized with acute pancreatitis develops numbness around the mouth and carpal spasms. Which laboratory value does the nurse anticipate is most likely abnormal?

options.text: Low serum calcium.
High serum sodium.
Low hematocrit.
Low chloride.

questions.rationale: Perioral numbness and carpal spasm suggest neuromuscular irritability from hypocalcemia. Acute pancreatitis can lower serum calcium as part of the disease process. Recognizing the pattern helps the nurse connect clinical signs with the expected electrolyte disturbance.

distractorRationales.b: This option overweights High serum sodium, although the nurse should be guided by Recognizing the pattern helps the nurse connect clinical signs with the expected electrolyte disturbance. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so Choosing it can delay neurologic safety assessment and leave neurologic deterioration unresolved.
distractorRationales.c: The problem with Low hematocrit is that it does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, although the nurse should be guided by Recognizing the pattern helps the nurse connect clinical signs with the expected electrolyte disturbance. Choosing it can delay neurologic safety assessment and leave neurologic deterioration unresolved.
distractorRationales.d: This distractor pulls attention toward Low chloride, when the bedside priority is driven by Acute pancreatitis can lower serum calcium as part of the disease process. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so Low serum calcium better matches the nursing priority, so this option would slow the needed intervention.

questions.takeaway: Tetany-type symptoms in pancreatitis point toward hypocalcemia.

questions.stem: A client performing peritoneal dialysis reports cloudy outflow and abdominal pain. Which action should the nurse take first?

options.text: Notify the provider because the findings suggest peritonitis.
Tell the client cloudy drainage is expected after dialysis.
Instruct the client to skip the next exchange.
Have the client drink extra water and wait until tomorrow.

questions.rationale: Cloudy dialysate and abdominal pain are classic findings of peritoneal dialysis-associated peritonitis. The nurse should escalate promptly for assessment and treatment. Reassurance or delay can worsen infection and threaten the dialysis access pathway.

distractorRationales.b: Selecting Tell the client cloudy drainage is expected after dialysis would be unsafe, rather than acting on Cloudy dialysate and abdominal pain are classic findings of peritoneal dialysis-associated peritonitis. Normalizes a finding that should change the nurse's priority, so That mismatch risks slower recognition of infection progression and postpones the needed nursing response.
distractorRationales.c: This answer choice treats Instruct the client to skip the next exchange as the key issue, rather than acting on Cloudy dialysate and abdominal pain are classic findings of peritoneal dialysis-associated peritonitis. Moves to teaching or reassurance before the immediate risk is stabilized, so That mismatch risks slower recognition of infection progression and postpones the needed nursing response.
distractorRationales.d: Have the client drink extra water and wait until tomorrow fits a lower-risk interpretation, despite the clinical warning in A client performing peritoneal dialysis reports cloudy outflow and abdominal pain. Treats an active warning sign as something safe to watch, so The safer answer is Notify the provider because the findings suggest peritonitis because delaying infection or sepsis escalation can worsen infection progression.

questions.takeaway: Cloudy peritoneal dialysis outflow should be treated as peritonitis until proven otherwise.

questions.stem: A pregnant client at 32 weeks reports painless bright red vaginal bleeding. Which nursing action is the priority?

options.text: Apply an external fetal monitor and avoid a vaginal examination.
Perform a sterile vaginal exam to assess dilation.
Instruct the client to walk in the hallway to check if the bleeding slows.
Prepare the client for a routine discharge if contractions are absent.

questions.rationale: Painless bright red bleeding in the third trimester suggests placenta previa. The nurse should assess maternal-fetal status while avoiding vaginal examination because digital exams can worsen hemorrhage when the placenta overlies the cervical os. Early recognition and protection from avoidable bleeding are the priority.

distractorRationales.b: The clinical error in Perform a sterile vaginal exam to assess dilation is that it does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, without accounting for Early recognition and protection from avoidable bleeding are the priority. That choice could make the nurse miss the window for bleeding-risk intervention while bleeding or anticoagulation injury progresses.
distractorRationales.c: The reasoning trap in Instruct the client to walk in the hallway to check if the bleeding slows is that it moves to a lower-acuity action before the client is clinically stable, without accounting for Early recognition and protection from avoidable bleeding are the priority. Apply an external fetal monitor and avoid a vaginal examination better matches the nursing priority, so this option would slow the needed intervention.
distractorRationales.d: This distractor pulls attention toward Prepare the client for a routine discharge if contractions are absent, without accounting for The nurse should assess maternal-fetal status while avoiding vaginal examination because digital exams can worsen hemorrhage when the. Treats an active warning sign as something safe to watch, so The correct path is Apply an external fetal monitor and avoid a vaginal examination, which matches the priority cues instead of a secondary concern.

questions.takeaway: Suspected placenta previa means monitor and protect; do not perform a vaginal exam.

questions.stem: A client 3 days after delivery calls the postpartum unit reporting a severe headache, visual spots, and swelling of the hands. Which instruction should the nurse give first?

options.text: Return to the hospital for immediate evaluation.
Take an over-the-counter pain reliever and rest in a dark room.
Limit fluids for the rest of the day.
Wait until the routine postpartum visit next week.

questions.rationale: Severe headache, visual changes, and edema after delivery raise concern for postpartum preeclampsia, which can progress to seizure or stroke. The client needs urgent evaluation rather than home symptom management. Delayed care can miss a serious hypertensive emergency.

distractorRationales.b: The problem with Take an over-the-counter pain reliever and rest in a dark room is that it does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, rather than acting on Severe headache, visual changes. That mismatch risks slower recognition of neurologic deterioration and postpones the needed nursing response.
distractorRationales.c: The problem with Limit fluids for the rest of the day is that it does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, rather than acting on Severe headache, visual changes. The safer answer is Return to the hospital for immediate evaluation because delaying neurologic safety assessment can worsen neurologic deterioration.
distractorRationales.d: This answer choice treats Wait until the routine postpartum visit next week as the key issue, rather than acting on Severe headache and visual changes after delivery should be treated as postpartum preeclampsia until proven otherwise. Treats an active warning sign as something safe to watch, so The safer answer is Return to the hospital for immediate evaluation because delaying neurologic safety assessment can worsen neurologic deterioration.

questions.takeaway: Severe headache and visual changes after delivery should be treated as postpartum preeclampsia until proven otherwise.

questions.stem: A client is 4 hours postoperative after a thyroidectomy and develops inspiratory stridor. Which action is the priority?

options.text: Prepare for emergency airway support and notify the provider immediately.
Offer ice chips to soothe the throat.
Document the finding as expected postoperative hoarseness.
Ask the client to speak loudly so the nurse can assess voice quality.

questions.rationale: Stridor after thyroid surgery may indicate airway obstruction from swelling, hematoma, or laryngeal nerve complications and requires immediate action. The nurse should treat it as an airway emergency rather than a routine sore-throat complaint. Delaying airway support can quickly become life-threatening.

distractorRationales.b: This option makes Offer ice chips to soothe the throat the focus, even though the higher-risk finding is Stridor after thyroid surgery may indicate airway obstruction from swelling, hematoma, or laryngeal nerve complications and requires immediate. distractorRationales.c: Document the finding as expected postoperative hoarseness is not the best answer, and misses that Post-thyroidectomy stridor is an airway emergency, not routine hoarseness. Normalizes a finding that should change the nurse's priority, so Prepare for emergency airway support and notify the provider immediately better matches the nursing priority, so this option would slow the needed intervention.
distractorRationales.d: This distractor pulls attention toward Ask the client to speak loudly so the nurse can assess voice quality, but the stem cue is Stridor after thyroid surgery may indicate airway obstruction from swelling, hematoma, or laryngeal nerve complications and requires immediate. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so Prepare for emergency airway support and notify the provider immediately better matches the nursing priority, so this option would slow the needed intervention.

questions.takeaway: Post-thyroidectomy stridor is an airway emergency, not routine hoarseness.

questions.stem: Which statement by a patient taking warfarin shows correct understanding?

options.text: "I will report unusual bleeding or bruising right away."
"I can double the dose if I miss one dose."
"I should stop the medication once I feel better."
"A green salad every day is unsafe no matter what."

questions.rationale: Warfarin can increase bleeding risk, so the patient should know to watch for and report bleeding signs. **Why the others are wrong:** - B Doubling a missed dose can be dangerous. - C Anticoagulants are not stopped casually. - D Vitamin K intake should be consistent, not completely avoided without guidance.

distractorRationales.b: "I can double the dose if I miss one dose." is not the best answer, but the stem cue is Anticoagulant teaching should focus on bleeding safety and consistency. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so It is less defensible because "I will report unusual bleeding or bruising right away." targets the problem most likely to deteriorate first.

Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so It is less defensible because "I will report unusual bleeding or bruising right away." targets the problem most likely to deteriorate first.
distractorRationales.d: "A green salad every day is unsafe no matter what." fits a lower-risk interpretation, rather than acting on Anticoagulant teaching should focus on bleeding safety and consistency. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so Choosing it can delay bleeding-risk intervention and leave bleeding or anticoagulation injury unresolved.

questions.takeaway: Anticoagulant teaching should focus on bleeding safety and consistency.

questions.stem: After a patient falls while attempting to ambulate unassisted, the nurse completes an incident report. Which statement about incident reports is correct?

options.text: The incident report should be referenced in the patient's medical record to ensure continuity of care
The incident report is an internal quality improvement tool; it is not referenced in the medical record and is not discoverable in litigation
Incident reports are part of the permanent medical record and must be provided to patients upon request
The nurse should document 'incident report filed' in the nursing notes to alert other providers

questions.rationale: Incident reports (occurrence reports) are internal quality improvement documents: they are NOT referenced in the patient's medical record. Referencing them in the chart (A, D) makes them discoverable in litigation, eliminating their protection as internal QI documents. The nurse documents the clinical facts in the medical record (what happened, patient response, assessment findings, interventions) without mentioning the incident report. In many states, incident reports prepared in anticipation of litigation may be protected from discovery, but only if not referenced in the chart. They are not part of the permanent medical record (C).

distractorRationales.a: Selecting The incident report should be referenced in the patient's medical record to ensure continuity of care would be unsafe, instead of responding to The nurse documents the clinical facts in the medical record (what happened, patient response, assessment findings, interventions) without. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so The nurse should prioritize The incident report is an internal quality improvement tool; it is not referenced in the medical; otherwise clinical safety risk remains the active threat.
distractorRationales.c: Selecting Incident reports are part of the permanent medical record and must be provided to patients upon would be unsafe, instead of responding to The nurse documents the clinical facts in the medical record (what happened, patient response, assessment findings, interventions) without. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so The nurse should prioritize The incident report is an internal quality improvement tool; it is not referenced in the medical; otherwise clinical safety risk remains the active threat.
distractorRationales.d: The nurse should document 'incident report filed' in the nursing notes to alert other providers is not the best answer, when the bedside priority is driven by The nurse documents the clinical facts in the medical record (what happened, patient response, assessment findings, interventions) without. Waits on escalation while the bedside priority remains unresolved, so The incident report is an internal quality improvement tool; it is not referenced in the medical is safer because it addresses clinical safety risk before routine care or reassurance.

questions.takeaway: Incident reports: do NOT reference in the medical record; document clinical facts of the event in the chart only — referencing the incident report makes it legally discoverable.
